mainEntity.name: How much does a BMW 4 Series 435i engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned 435i (N55) engine typically costs £4,000–£8,500 supply only. Fitting adds £900–£2,000 depending on workshop location. Main dealer new engines can exceed £9,000 supply only. Used engines typically range from £2,500–£4,500, with labour adding £800–£3,000. Use our calculator for a figure specific to your car.

mainEntity.name: Is the BMW 4 Series 435i engine reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The N55 is one of BMW's more reliable modern engines. It's far more robust than the twin-turbo N54 it replaced and doesn't suffer the timing chain issues of BMW's four-cylinder engines. However, it has known weak points: the plastic charge pipe is prone to cracking, wastegate rattle is common, and gaskets leak with age. With proper maintenance, these engines can exceed 150,000 miles.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the BMW 4 Series 435i?

acceptedAnswer.text: Charge pipe failure (factory plastic unit cracks under boost), wastegate rattle (rattling on cold start and deceleration), oil leaks from valve cover and oil filter housing gaskets, and electric water pump failure (especially over 80,000 miles). Timing chain issues are less common on the N55 than on earlier BMW engines.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a BMW 435i engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Apply the 70% BMW scrap threshold: if your 435i is worth £14,000 and a recon engine is £6,000 (43%), it's worth it. If the vehicle is a tired £8,000 example, the same engine exceeds 70% — get a scrap valuation first. The 435i's strong enthusiast following and retained value often justify replacement spend.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my BMW 4 Series 435i engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: The engine code is listed on the VIN plate and vehicle documentation. The 435i (2013–2016) uses the N55B30O0 — a 3.0-litre turbocharged inline-six producing 320 bhp. It was fitted to F32 (Coupé), F33 (Convertible), and F36 (Gran Coupé) chassis. Tell us your registration date and we'll confirm it for free.

mainEntity.name: Can I repair the charge pipe, or does it need replacement?

acceptedAnswer.text: The factory plastic charge pipe can be replaced with an OEM unit (£150–£400), but many owners upgrade to an aftermarket aluminium charge pipe (£300–£600) as a preventative measure. The aluminium unit will not crack under boost and is a worthwhile investment for any N55-powered car.

replacementCosts.figuresNote: Figures above reflect the only engine generation this badge ever used — the N55B30O0. See the Era Map above for era-specific pricing. Used engine costs typically range from £2,000 to £12,000 depending on model and mileage, with labour adding £800–£3,000.
replacementCosts.labourEstimate: Fitting a 435i engine typically takes 10–14 hours at £65–£95/hr (independent). The N55 is a well-understood engine, but access can be tight in the 4 Series engine bay.

cta.label: → Compare 435i engine prices from UK specialists

problems.title: Charge Pipe Failure (N55)
problems.symptoms: Sudden loss of power, drivetrain malfunction warning, boost leak, check engine light, audible pop or hiss from the engine bay.
problems.typicalMileage: Any mileage — the factory plastic charge pipe is a known weak point and can fail at any time.
problems.replacementCost: N/A — repair only; if ignored, the car will run in limp mode with significantly reduced power.

urgency.label: Check
urgency.text: book inspection urgently if symptoms appear.

problems.recommendation: The factory plastic charge pipe is notorious for cracking under boost pressure — post after post about charge pipes on these engines cracking and failing, even on stock tunes. Many owners upgrade to an aftermarket aluminium charge pipe as a preventative measure. If you experience a sudden loss of power or a drivetrain malfunction warning, this is one of the first things to check.

problems.title: Wastegate Rattle (Turbocharger)
problems.symptoms: Rattling noise from the engine bay on cold start, rattling upon deceleration, loss of power, check engine light (P30FF), poor fuel economy.
problems.typicalMileage: 40,000–80,000 miles — can appear earlier with aggressive driving.
problems.replacementCost: N/A — repair only if caught early; severe wastegate wear may require full turbo replacement.

urgency.label: Check
urgency.text: book inspection urgently if symptoms appear.

problems.recommendation: Wastegate rattle is a common issue on the N55, particularly on earlier E-Series models with pneumatic wastegates. The wastegate bushing wears over time, causing excessive movement and rattling. While largely cosmetic, it can be addressed with upgraded wastegate actuators. If you hear a rattle on cold start or deceleration, have the turbocharger inspected.

problems.title: Oil Leaks (Valve Cover & Oil Filter Housing Gaskets)
problems.symptoms: Burning oil smell, visible oil on the engine or garage floor, low oil level warnings, smoke from the engine bay.
problems.typicalMileage: 60,000–100,000 miles — age-related as gaskets harden and deteriorate.
problems.replacementCost: N/A — repair only; if ignored, oil leaks can lead to oil starvation or damage to other components.

urgency.label: Monitor
urgency.text: book inspection soon if symptoms appear.

problems.recommendation: The N55 is prone to oil leaks from the valve cover gasket and oil filter housing gasket. Oil almost always leaks from one side of the valve cover and drips onto the exhaust manifold — you'll notice this by a burning oil smell. The valve cover itself can also warp or develop micro cracks with age, so it's often recommended to replace the entire cover, not just the gasket. If you notice a burning oil smell or visible oil, have the gaskets inspected promptly.

rows.whenItMakesSense: Simple fix — upgrade to aluminium for peace of mind
Adjustment or rebuild cheaper than full turbo replacement
Common maintenance item — replace entire cover, not just gasket
Preventative — replace before oil loss causes damage
Common at 80,000+ miles — replace with thermostat
Full engine replacement required if bottom-end or valvetrain fails

buyingChecks.title: Buying This 435i? Check These First
buyIf: * Full service history with regular oil changes (every 8,000–10,000 miles)
* Charge pipe replaced with an aftermarket aluminium unit
* No wastegate rattle on cold start or deceleration
* Water pump and thermostat replaced (especially over 80,000 miles)
avoidIf: * Plastic charge pipe still fitted — it will fail eventually
* Wastegate rattle or P30FF code present
* Evidence of oil leaks from valve cover or oil filter housing
* Poor service history — the N55 is sensitive to oil quality

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The N55-powered 435i is one of the sweet spots in BMW's modern lineup — a reliable, tuneable, and characterful inline-six. It's far more robust than the N54 it replaced and doesn't suffer the timing chain nightmares of BMW's four-cylinder engines. However, it has its weak points: the charge pipe will fail, wastegate rattle is common, and gaskets will leak with age. A £6,000 reconditioned engine on a 435i valued at £14,000 is 43% of the car's value — worth it for a well-maintained example. With the 70% BMW scrap threshold, the 435i's strong retained value makes engine replacement a viable option.

cta.label: → Compare 435i engine prices before you buy

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used BMW 4 Series 435i (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: * Cold Start — Listen for rattling (wastegate rattle is common on the N55)
* Charge Pipe — Check if the factory plastic charge pipe has been replaced with aluminium
* Service History — Regular oil changes every 8,000–10,000 miles using BMW-approved oil
* Oil Leaks — Check for oil around the valve cover and oil filter housing
* Smoke Check — Blue smoke on startup could indicate turbo seal wear
* Water Pump — Ask if the electric water pump and thermostat have been replaced (especially over 80,000 miles)
* Engine Warning Light — Have codes been read? Any P30FF (wastegate) codes?
* MOT History — Check for emissions-related failures
